title: Record of Meeting Summary of Discussions Among Members of the Community Depository Institutions Advisory Council
summary: The Community Depository Institutions Advisory Council (CDIAC) met on April 11, 2024, providing a qualitative snapshot of regional economic conditions. Council members reported that the economy remains resilient but with persistent inflation pressures, particularly affecting lower-income households. Labor costs are moderating from 6-7% increases last year to 4-5% this year, while goods inflation is slowing but services inflation persists due to labor intensity.
key_points: Economy remains surprisingly resilient despite persistent inflationary pressures, with bifurcation between upper- and lower-income groups
Inflation has moderated from peak but remains stubbornly high, driven by labor costs and strong consumer demand; Council members expressed doubts about near-term feasibility of 2% target
Goods inflation is slowing due to inventory buildup and unsold auto inventory, but services inflation persists due to labor costs
Technology costs have increased significantly from 2-3% averages to as much as 10% in 2024
Labor costs have moderated from 6-7% average increases last year to 4-5% this year; blue-collar labor remains challenging to find while white-collar talent pool is larger
Housing prices remain restrictive but rate of increase is slowing; affordability issues are regionalized with some areas seeing loosening supply conditions
Council expressed cautious optimism regarding economic outlook given stable consumer confidence and slow return of small business capital investment
why_it_matters: This CDIAC meeting summary provides qualitative, regional intelligence on economic conditions from depository institution representatives. While not official FOMC deliberations, it offers ground-level insight into how financial institutions perceive inflation persistence, labor market tightness, and housing affordability—key inputs for Fed policy considerations. The moderation in wage growth and goods inflation aligns with Fed's disinflation narrative, while persistent services inflation and housing costs explain ongoing policy caution.
macro_context: The CDIAC report provides anecdotal evidence of economic conditions as of April 2024. Key macro implications include: (1) goods-side disinflation is materializing as expected, (2) services inflation remains sticky due to labor-intensive nature, (3) labor market is gradually cooling with wage growth moderating from 6-7% to 4-5%, (4) housing affordability remains a challenge despite price moderation. This qualitative evidence supports the narrative that the Fed was navigating between weakening inflation and resilient economic activity. NOTE: This is consultative advisory council input, not official FOMC deliberations or formal Fed policy output.
market_structure_context: CDIAC serves as a consultative body providing regional, ground-level perspective from community depository institutions. This intelligence complements formal data releases by offering qualitative color on how financial institutions perceive economic conditions in their districts—information that may inform but does not constitute official Fed policy positions.

Source was truncated; full text may contain additional relevant discussion.
